questions.stem: An ecosystem is best defined as which of the following?
options: A community of living organisms together with their non-living environment, interacting as a system.
Only the rocks and soil of an area.
A list of species with no interactions.
questions.explanation: An ecosystem consists of a community of living organisms together with the non-living, physical environment with which they interact. It includes both biotic and abiotic components linked by flows of energy and matter. A single organism or the soil alone does not constitute an ecosystem.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Which of the following is an example of a biotic factor in an ecosystem?
questions.explanation: Biotic factors are the living components of an ecosystem, such as plants, animals, fungi, and bacteria. Temperature, rainfall, and sunlight are abiotic, meaning non-living, physical factors. The distinction between biotic and abiotic is fundamental to describing ecosystems.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Which of the following is an example of an abiotic factor in an ecosystem?
questions.explanation: Abiotic factors are the non-living, physical and chemical components of an ecosystem, such as soil pH, temperature, light, water, and minerals. Antelope, grasses, and fungi are all living organisms and so are biotic. Soil pH is the only non-living option listed.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: In an ecosystem, organisms that make their own food by photosynthesis, such as green plants, occupy which role?
questions.explanation: Producers, also called autotrophs, make their own food, typically by photosynthesis, and form the base of the food chain. Green plants and algae are the main producers in most ecosystems. Consumers and decomposers depend, directly or indirectly, on the organic matter that producers create.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: A herbivore that eats plants directly occupies which trophic level?
questions.explanation: A primary consumer is an organism that feeds directly on producers, so a plant-eating herbivore is a primary consumer. Secondary consumers eat the primary consumers, and so on up the chain. Producers make their own food, while decomposers break down dead matter.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: A carnivore that eats a herbivore occupies which trophic level?
questions.explanation: A secondary consumer feeds on primary consumers, so a carnivore that eats a plant-eating herbivore is a secondary consumer. The herbivore it eats is the primary consumer, and the plant the herbivore ate is the producer. Each step away from the producers defines a higher trophic level.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Organisms such as many bacteria and fungi that break down dead organic matter and return nutrients to the environment are called which of the following?
questions.explanation: Decomposers, including many bacteria and fungi, break down dead organisms and waste, releasing nutrients back into the soil and environment. This recycling makes the nutrients available again to producers. Without decomposers, nutrients would remain locked in dead matter.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: A food chain shows which of the following?
options: Only the non-living factors of an ecosystem.
The total mass of the atmosphere.
A single linear pathway of who eats whom, from producers up through consumers.
questions.explanation: A food chain is a single, linear sequence showing how energy and nutrients pass from one organism to the next as one is eaten by another, starting with producers. It is a simplified picture of feeding relationships. A food web combines many interlinked food chains to give a fuller picture.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: How does a food web differ from a single food chain?
options: A food web is a network of many interconnected food chains.
A food web contains no producers.
A food web shows only one predator and one prey.
A food web describes only abiotic factors.
questions.explanation: A food web is a network of many interconnected food chains, reflecting the fact that most organisms eat, and are eaten by, more than one kind of organism. It gives a more realistic picture of feeding relationships than a single chain. Food webs still begin with producers at their base.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: What is the ultimate source of energy for most ecosystems on Earth?
questions.explanation: The Sun is the ultimate source of energy for most ecosystems, since producers capture sunlight through photosynthesis and convert it into chemical energy in food. This energy then flows through consumers and decomposers. Wind and rocks are not primary energy sources for food chains.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: According to the ten percent law of energy transfer, roughly what fraction of the energy at one trophic level is passed on to the next?
questions.explanation: The ten percent law states that on average only about 10 percent of the energy stored at one trophic level is passed on to the next. The rest is lost mainly as heat through respiration and through incomplete consumption and digestion. This loss is why food chains usually have only a few links.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Why does most energy fail to pass from one trophic level to the next?
options: Because energy moves backward down the chain.
Because producers use no energy at all.
Because energy is created at each level.
Because much energy is lost as heat through respiration and is not all consumed or digested.
questions.explanation: Most energy is not passed on because organisms use much of it for their own life processes and lose it as heat through respiration, while some food is never eaten or fully digested. As a result only a small fraction, around a tenth, is available to the next level. Energy is not created at each level; it is transformed and partly lost.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: If producers in an ecosystem capture 10000 units of energy, roughly how much is available to the primary consumers, according to the ten percent law?
questions.explanation: By the ten percent law, about 10 percent of 10000 units passes to the next level, giving roughly $0.10 \times 10000 = 1000$ units for the primary consumers. The remaining energy is lost mainly as heat and through unconsumed material. Applying the same rule again would leave about 100 units for the next level up.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: An ecological pyramid that shows the amount of energy available at each trophic level is called which of the following?
questions.explanation: A pyramid of energy is an ecological pyramid that shows the amount of energy available at each successive trophic level, and it always narrows upward because energy is lost between levels. Other ecological pyramids show numbers of organisms or biomass. There is no pyramid of rainfall or temperature.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Why is a pyramid of energy always upright, narrowing from the base to the top?
options: Because the top level has the most energy.
Because energy increases at each higher trophic level.
Because energy decreases at each higher trophic level due to losses between levels.
Because all levels contain exactly the same energy.
questions.explanation: A pyramid of energy is always upright because the energy available decreases at each higher trophic level, as only about a tenth is passed on and the rest is lost as heat. The base, occupied by producers, therefore holds the most energy. This steady decline guarantees the upright shape.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: In the nitrogen cycle, the conversion of atmospheric nitrogen gas into ammonia or related compounds that organisms can use is called which process?
questions.explanation: Nitrogen fixation is the conversion of relatively inert atmospheric nitrogen gas into ammonia or related compounds that living organisms can use. It is carried out by certain bacteria, some living free in the soil and others in association with plant roots, as well as by lightning. This step makes nitrogen available to the rest of the cycle.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Which group of organisms is chiefly responsible for nitrogen fixation in natural ecosystems?
questions.explanation: Certain bacteria are chiefly responsible for nitrogen fixation, converting atmospheric nitrogen into forms that organisms can use. Some are free-living in the soil, while others live in nodules on the roots of legumes. Plants, mammals, and fish cannot fix nitrogen by themselves.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: In the nitrogen cycle, denitrification refers to which process?
options: The conversion of nitrates back into nitrogen gas, returning it to the atmosphere.
The fixation of nitrogen gas into ammonia.
The uptake of nitrates by plant roots.
questions.explanation: Denitrification is the conversion of nitrates in the soil back into nitrogen gas, which returns to the atmosphere, and it is carried out by certain bacteria. It effectively closes the nitrogen cycle by balancing fixation. It is the reverse, in effect, of nitrogen entering the soil as usable compounds.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: In the carbon cycle, which process removes carbon dioxide from the atmosphere and incorporates the carbon into organic compounds?
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis removes carbon dioxide from the atmosphere and uses the carbon to build organic compounds such as glucose in producers. This is the main way carbon enters living systems. Respiration and combustion do the opposite, releasing carbon dioxide back into the atmosphere.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: In the carbon cycle, which processes return carbon dioxide to the atmosphere?
questions.explanation: Respiration by living organisms and the combustion, or burning, of wood and fossil fuels both release carbon dioxide back into the atmosphere. These processes balance the removal of carbon dioxide by photosynthesis. The freezing of water and nitrogen fixation are not part of how carbon returns to the air.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: What general role do the carbon and nitrogen cycles play in ecosystems?
options: They recycle essential elements between living organisms and the environment.
They create new matter from nothing.
They have no effect on living organisms.
questions.explanation: Nutrient cycles such as the carbon and nitrogen cycles recycle essential chemical elements between living organisms and the non-living environment. Atoms are used, released, and reused rather than created or destroyed. This continual recycling keeps a limited supply of nutrients available to support life.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Which statement contrasts the flow of energy with the cycling of nutrients in an ecosystem?
options: Neither energy nor nutrients move through the ecosystem.
Energy flows through the ecosystem and is ultimately lost as heat, while nutrients are recycled.
Both energy and nutrients are recycled endlessly with no loss.
Energy is recycled, while nutrients flow through once and are lost.
questions.explanation: Energy enters as sunlight, passes through trophic levels, and is ultimately lost from the ecosystem as heat, so it must be continually resupplied. Nutrients, by contrast, are recycled between organisms and the environment through cycles such as the carbon and nitrogen cycles. This difference, energy flowing through while matter cycles, is a central principle of ecology.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Which of the following correctly orders a simple food chain from its base upward?
questions.explanation: A food chain begins with a producer and moves up through consumers. Grass is the producer, the grasshopper is a primary consumer that eats it, and the frog is a secondary consumer that eats the grasshopper. This gives the order grass, then grasshopper, then frog.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Plants take up nitrogen from the soil mainly in which form?
options: They do not take up nitrogen at all.
As nitrogen gas drawn in through their leaves.
As nitrates absorbed through their roots.
questions.explanation: Plants take up nitrogen mainly as nitrates, and to some extent ammonium, absorbed through their roots from the soil. They cannot use nitrogen gas directly, which is why nitrogen fixation by bacteria is so important. The nitrogen is then built into proteins and other molecules within the plant.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Classify each component of an ecosystem by choosing the correct option for each blank.
questions.solution: The living components of an ecosystem, such as plants and animals, are the {{1}} factors. The non-living components, such as temperature and rainfall, are the {{2}} factors. Organisms that make their own food by photosynthesis and form the base of the food chain are the {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Living components are biotic factors and non-living components are abiotic factors. Producers make their own food by photosynthesis and form the base of the food chain.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: A pyramid of energy starts with producers that capture 10000 units of energy. Apply the ten percent law to the next levels.
questions.solution: By the ten percent law, the primary consumers receive about ten percent of the producers' energy, which is $0.10 \times 10000$ units, equal to {{1}} units. Applying the same rule again, the secondary consumers receive about ten percent of that, which equals {{2}} units.
questions.explanation: About 10 percent of 10000 units gives 1000 units for the primary consumers. Applying the ten percent law again gives about 100 units for the secondary consumers.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Identify the processes of the nitrogen cycle by choosing the correct option for each blank.
questions.solution: The conversion of atmospheric nitrogen gas into ammonia or related usable compounds is called nitrogen {{1}}, and it is carried out mainly by certain {{2}}. The conversion of nitrates in the soil back into nitrogen gas, returning it to the atmosphere, is called {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Nitrogen fixation converts nitrogen gas into usable compounds and is carried out mainly by bacteria. Denitrification converts nitrates back into nitrogen gas, returning it to the atmosphere.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Complete the description of the carbon cycle and energy flow by choosing the correct option for each blank.
questions.solution: In the carbon cycle, carbon dioxide is removed from the atmosphere by {{1}} and incorporated into organic compounds. It is returned to the atmosphere by respiration and by the {{2}} of fuels. Across an ecosystem, energy ultimately {{3}} through the system and is lost as heat, while nutrients are recycled.
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis removes carbon dioxide from the atmosphere, while respiration and combustion return it. Energy flows through an ecosystem and is lost as heat, whereas nutrients are recycled.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)
